step_by_step_thinking:
The McRoberts maneuver involves flexing the thighs sharply over the abdomen, which increases tension on the pelvic floor structures and potentially compresses certain nerves. Among the given options, the obturator nerve innervates muscles in the medial aspect of the thigh and passes through the adductor canal before entering the pelvis. When the legs are suddenly hyperflexed during the McRoberts maneuver, there's increased compression of the obturator nerve due to its position near the anterior pelvic wall. While some sources mention neural involvement in hip movements, none specifically link sudden hyperflexion to any of the listed nerves except possibly the obturator nerve. Therefore, based on anatomical knowledge and typical associations with maneuvers involving leg positioning, option B — Obturator nerve — best fits the scenario described.
\boxed{B} To clarify: although no explicit statement about McRoberts maneuver was included in the retrieved evidence, our conclusion relies on standard anatomical principles regarding nerve anatomy relative to joint motion and positioning. raw_response:
The McRoberts maneuver involves flexing the thighs sharply over the abdomen, which increases tension on the pelvic floor structures and potentially compresses certain nerves. Among the given options, the obturator nerve innervates muscles in the medial aspect of the thigh and passes through the adductor canal before entering the pelvis. When the legs are suddenly hyperflexed during the McRoberts maneuver, there's increased compression of the obturator nerve due to its position near the anterior pelvic wall. While some sources mention neural involvement in hip movements, none specifically link sudden hyperflexion to any of the listed nerves except possibly the obturator nerve. Therefore, based on anatomical knowledge and typical associations with maneuvers involving leg positioning, option B — Obturator nerve — best fits the scenario described.
\boxed{B} To clarify: although no explicit statement about McRoberts maneuver was included in the retrieved evidence, our conclusion relies on standard anatomical principles regarding nerve anatomy relative to joint motion and positioning.
